articles.title: Childhood Computing
articles.summary: Childhood Computing By Susam Pal on 24 May 2026 I recently stumbled upon a blog post titled Childhood Computing that made me think about my own childhood computing experience. Like the author, I have for most of my life been fascinated by computers and have grown up in a tiny industrial town where we had access to a computer lab with IBM PC compatible machines. The lab was very old, mostly monochrome CRT monitors with no hard disks or storage, and everything was performed through a ritual involving floppy disk loading and physical notebook saving. This experience laid the foundation for my early programming endeavors, including writing Logo programs on paper that were later copied and modified by classmates in the lab.

articles.title: Debian SE Linux and PinTheft
articles.summary: Debian's SE Linux system has been compromised by a new exploit called PinTheft, which allows attackers to steal sensitive data from the system. The exploit works by exploiting a vulnerability in the user_t context, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code as the root user. This is particularly concerning because SE Linux provides a high level of security and isolation between the kernel and user space, making it difficult for attackers to gain access to sensitive data.

articles.title: A Network Allow-List Won't Stop Exfiltration
articles.summary: A Network Allow-List Won't Stop Exfiltration is a technical article discussing how network allow-lists can fail to prevent exfiltration, specifically when it comes to DNS lookups and data flowing through authorized channels. The problem lies in the fact that many sandbox solutions rely on domain allow-lists, which are not specific to each application or service, but rather a general policy that allows all domains. This means that even if an application is running within a sandbox with a network policy, it can still exfiltrate sensitive data through authorized channels such as DNS lookups.

articles.title: Alexander Grothendieck Revolutionized 20th-Century Mathematics
articles.summary: Alexander Grothendieck revolutionized 20th-century mathematics by pushing the field to a new level of abstraction, focusing on the relationships between objects rather than their individual properties. His work in algebraic geometry centered around revealing hidden geometric structure in shapes defined by polynomial equations and sets of many equations. This innovative approach has had a lasting impact on the discipline, influencing mathematicians for decades.

articles.title: “Long-Term Support” doesn’t mean what you think
articles.summary: The concept of "Long-Term Support" (LTS) releases is often misunderstood as meaning a product will remain unchanged for an extended period. However, the term refers specifically to Debian Stable and Ubuntu's five-year support promise, which includes security updates and maintenance for three years after each stable release. This means that each discrete OS release continues receiving updates for a certain number of years, with fixes for security issues being prioritized over new features or UI improvements.

articles.title: .NET (OK, C#) finally gets union types
articles.summary: Unions are a basic data structure in functional programming languages that allow having a single type represent two different things. In .NET 11, unions are finally here and can be used to combine any arbitrary set of types into one. This feature is ideal for representing data that could be one of several potentially unrelated types, such as three record types containing different properties. With the union keyword, developers can create a single type that represents all possible combinations of these types, providing improved safety and flexibility compared to previous methods.

articles.title: On the (2021)
articles.summary: The description list element is underrated as it's used to represent a list of name-value pairs and is incredibly versatile, making it suitable for various UI patterns such as listing lodging amenities or individual charges in monthly rent. The anatomy of the description list includes three separate elements: <dl>, <dt> for the name-value pair, and <dd> for the detail. This semantic way to mark up a list of name-value groups is essential for developers who want to create accessible and user-friendly interfaces.

articles.title: Jira is Turing-Complete
articles.summary: Jira is Turing-Complete, a claim that has been folklore for some time, is now being proven through a proof of its computational model, which uses two unbounded counters and finite set of labeled instructions to simulate the Minsky register machine, a Turing-complete model established by Minsky in 1967. This achievement demonstrates Jira's automation language can reduce any program into this model, making it Turing-complete.

The issue arose when the service became overloaded and failed requests were retried with a short timeout, leading to increased load on the system. To address this, the developer turned to Go's panic mechanism for non-local error handling, which allows functions like sort to return errors that can be propagated through the call stack.
